front: What is the basic operation and worst-case time complexity of Selection Sort?
back: The basic operation is comparisons. The worst-case time complexity is O(n²), as it always makes n(n-1)/2 comparisons regardless of the input array's order.

front: How does Bubble Sort work, and what is its key characteristic?
back: Bubble Sort repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. This process is repeated until the list is sorted. A key characteristic is that after each pass, the next largest element 'bubbles up' to its correct position at the end of the list.

front: What is the main advantage of Selection Sort over other simple sorting algorithms?
back: Its main advantage is that it minimizes the number of swaps. It performs only O(n) swaps in the worst case, making it useful when the cost of swapping items is high (e.g., writing to disk).

front: Under what condition can Bubble Sort achieve its best-case time complexity of O(n), and why?
back: This occurs when the input array is already sorted. An optimized version of Bubble Sort can detect that no swaps were made in a pass and terminate early, requiring only n-1 comparisons in a single pass.

front: Are Selection Sort and Bubble Sort stable sorting algorithms?
back: No, Selection Sort is not stable because it may change the relative order of equal elements when it swaps the minimum element into place. A standard implementation of Bubble Sort is stable because it only swaps adjacent elements when they are out of order, preserving the relative order of equal keys.
